summary: Gruber highlights Ken Segall's frustration after The Guardian replaced his nuanced headline with a sensationalized clickbait version that contradicted his actual argument. Segall's original piece asked whether Apple had lost its simplicity, but The Guardian declared it as fact. Gruber argues that even readers who are aware of clickbait still have their interpretation of articles shaped by provocative headlines. He laments that nearly every publication, even reputable ones, now uses tabloid-style headlines, calling it a scourge of modern media.
key_points: The Guardian changed Segall's questioning headline into a declarative clickbait statement that misrepresented his argument
Even readers who consciously try to discount clickbait headlines are still influenced by them in how they interpret articles
The print-era distinction between serious publications and tabloids has collapsed online, with nearly all outlets using sensational headlines
Writers publishing in other outlets should insist on headline approval to protect their arguments from editorial distortion
money_quotes: Everyone knows what clickbait is, but even though we, collectively, are aware of it and presumably attempt to defend against it by not taking headlines at face value, it's really amazing how much a provocative headline can affect the interpretation of a non-provocative article.
Back in the print era, we knew the difference between serious publications and tabloids, and adjusted our trust accordingly.
But today almost every publication, even good ones, use tabloid-style sensational headlines.
I don't do much writing for other publications these days, but at this point I would insist on approval over the headline.
key_insight: Clickbait headlines are corrosive because they distort readers' interpretation of articles even when those readers know they should discount them.
